straight_beat.name: Chet Pazulski
straight_beat.column:
The Padres didn’t just beat the Pirates yesterday—they did it with the kind of efficiency that makes you wonder if the Pirates’ offense is still learning how to speak English. San Diego’s 8-2 dismantling of Pittsburgh wasn’t just another loss; it was a masterclass in how a team with a competent pitching staff and a lineup that occasionally remembers to swing the bat can dominate. The Pirates, meanwhile, looked like a team trying to figure out if they’re supposed to hit or field.  

Let’s start with the pitching. Kyle Gibson didn’t just pitch; he delivered a performance that made you think the Pirates’ rotation might finally have a plan. Seven innings, nine strikeouts, two walks—Gibson’s line looked like something you’d see on a highlight reel. Meanwhile, the Pirates’ starters, who’ve been a mixed bag all season, looked like they

optimist.name: Franny Harsanyi
optimist.column:
The Pirates lost yesterday, but you know what? They’re still out there, swinging the bat, throwing the ball, and proving that baseball is a game of moments. Paul Skenes threw 102 pitches yesterday, and I almost called my daughter to come over and watch. That’s the feeling that comes back. That’s why we keep showing up.  

Now, I know the scoreboard says Padres 8, Pirates 2, but let’s not forget what we saw from Skenes. He’s got that Blass-Burgess thing going on—grit, heart, and a fastball that makes you think twice. Sure, he didn’t dominate like he did in the minors, but that’s the thing about growing pains. You don’t get to the big leagues without a few stumbles. And if you’re Skenes, you’ve got the kind of talent that turns stumbles into stories.  

The front office has been busy, too. They’re building something here, stacking the roster with young arms and sluggers who’ve got the look of future stars. You can’t ignore the 7-5 record either. One game back in the NL Central isn’t a death sentence—it’s a warning shot. The Pirates are in a race, and races are won by teams that keep their heads up.  

I’ll be

pessimist.name: Wally Horvath
pessimist.column:
The Pirates lost again. Another game, another defeat, another reminder that this team is stuck in a loop of mediocrity. San Diego trounced us 8-2, and while the Padres’ offense lit up the scoreboard, our own lineup looked like a group of kids playing baseball with a broken bat. The Pirates are 7-5, but that record doesn’t tell the story of a team that’s been limping along since the days of Forbes Field. We’re 1.0 games behind in the NL Central, and if you’re keeping score, that’s not exactly a sign of progress. It’s a sign of stagnation.  

The pitching staff? A revolving door of mediocrity. Skenes pitched the game of his life yesterday, and yet the Pirates still lost. You’d think a guy who can dominate a lineup would be able to keep his team in a game, but no—Nutting’s luxury box must have been filled with peanuts, because the team’s fate seems to be sealed before the first pitch. Skenes’ performance was a flash of brilliance, but it’s not enough. The bullpen? A joke. The starters? A liability. You can’t build a playoff team on flashes of brilliance and a bullpen that looks like it’s being held together by duct tape.  

And the offense? Where’s the power? Where’s the heart? We’re hitting .240, and that’s not even close to good enough. The Pirates need to score runs, and they need to
